chunks.page_summary: This page describes the architecture and steps for monitoring multiple Rheonics sensors for fluid diagnostics using a USR-W610 converter, Node-RED, and Ignition SCADA.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Monitoring multiple Sensors using USR-W610, Node-RED and Ignition SCADA

Modified on: Wed, 22 Oct, 2025 at 4:16 PM

What products are involved?
This article is based on using multiple Rheonics sensors and the USR-W610 to send various sensor data through Wi-Fi to an MQTT Broker using Node-RED and visualize it remotely.

What is the purpose of this article?
This article demonstrates how to integrate the USR-W610 RS232/RS485 to Wi-Fi & Ethernet Converter with multiple Rheonics sensors simultaneously in Ignition SCADA.

TABLE OF CONTENTS
1. Overview
2. USR-W610 Setup
3. Sensor Module Electronics (SME) Configuration
4. Node-RED
   4.1. MQTT Topic Naming Convention
5. Configuring Ignition SCADA MQTT
6. Visualization in Ignition Designer
7. References

1. Overview
The remote visualization of multiple sensor data is utilized for fluid monitoring and diagnostics across different locations. This section demonstrates how to connect and publish data from several Rheonics sensors using the USR-W610 to connect to a Wi-Fi network and send the data through Node-RED to an MQTT broker. The following diagram presents the architecture with multiple sensors using the USR-W610 converter.

[Image: Diagram showing an architecture for sensor monitoring]
Figure 1: Architecture of multiple Rheonics sensors, USR-W610, Node-RED and Ignition SCADA integration

2. USR-W610 Setup
The USR-W610 module is used to send data to a Wi-Fi network. In this case, the device is configured as STA, so it is connected to the same Wi-Fi network as the device with Node-RED.
To see more information about the device setup refer to Connecting Rheonics Sensors to Wi-Fi Networks using USR-W610 (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/a)

3. Sensor Module Electronics (SME) Configuration
Each SME needs to have a different Modbus Address, in this case 4 SMEs are used to read 4 sensors, so the addresses are configured differently. This can be configured in the Rheonics Control Panel. Refer to the Modbus RTU Manual found in Manuals (https://es.rheonics.com/recursos/manuales/)
key_facts: The article focuses on using Rheonics sensors and the USR-W610 to send data via Wi-Fi to an MQTT Broker using Node-RED for remote visualization.
The purpose of this article is to demonstrate the integration of the USR-W610 RS232/RS485 to Wi-Fi & Ethernet Converter with multiple Rheonics sensors in Ignition SCADA.
The remote visualization of sensor data supports fluid monitoring and diagnostics across various locations.
The USR-W610 connects Rheonics sensors to a Wi-Fi network and facilitates data transmission through Node-RED to an MQTT broker.
The USR-W610 module is configured as a Station (STA) to connect to the Wi-Fi network.
Each Sensor Module Electronics (SME) requires a unique Modbus Address.
The example configuration uses 4 SMEs to read 4 sensors.
Modbus addresses for SMEs are configured via the Rheonics Control Panel.
diagram_labels: Rheonics Sensors (eg. SRV, SRC, DVP, otherse) for density, viscosity, temperature

chunks.page_summary: This page details the configuration of Modbus addresses within the Rheonics Control Panel and provides instructions for integrating Rheonics sensors (SME, SRV, SRD) into Node-RED using Modbus-Read nodes for data acquisition and publishing.

Figure 2: Modbus Address configuration in Rheonics Control Panel

4. Node-RED

(https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000419359-rheonics-sensor-integration-with-ipc-usr-w610-and-ignition-scada) To learn the basic steps for setting up Node-RED

To integrate more sensors in Node-RED, add a new Modbus client for each SME, assigning each its respective Modbus Address. In this example, parameters from three SRV sensors and one SRD sensor are read, each configured with the same IP address configured in the USR-W610 but with a different Unit-ID. Each Modbus-Read node in Node-RED is paired with a corresponding MQTT-out node to publish the sensor data.

1. Add a Modbus-Read node

Modbus - Read
Figure 3: Modbus-Read node

2. Configure the node with the following to read viscosity. The rest of the input parameters can be found in Modbus Input Registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297459-modbus-input-registers)
  * Name: e.g. viscosity
  * FC: FC4: Read Input Registers
  * Address: 40
  * Quantity: 2
  * Poll rate: 3

Edit Modbus-Read node
Delete Cancel Done
Properties
Settings Optionals
Name viscosity
Topic Topic
Unit-Id
FC FC 4: Read Input Registers
Address 40
Quantity 2
Poll Rate 3 second(s)
Delay to activate input
Server RTU_SRV1

Figure 4: Modbus-Read node configuration

3. On the Modbus-Read node, click on the Server field and press the plus to add a new Modbus client. In this case, it corresponds to one of the SME. In the Setting Tab, configure the following:
  * Type: TCP
  * Host: IP established in the USR-W610
  * Port: 502 (default)
  * Unit ID: Modbus Address of the SME
  * Timeout: 4000
  * Reconnect on timeout: (checked checkbox)
key_facts: The Rheonics Control Panel 5.5.2.5 allows configuration of Modbus RTU, LCD display settings, Ethernet parameters, Location ID, and displays Bluetooth LTE 4.0 MAC address.

The Operation History section of the control panel provides real-time clock (RTC) time, last power reset time, current supply voltage, SME temperature, and maximum recorded sensor and SME temperatures.
For integrating multiple sensors in Node-RED, a distinct Modbus client must be added for each SME sensor.
The example demonstrates reading parameters from three SRV sensors and one SRD sensor, each configured with the same IP address via USR-W610 but distinct Unit-IDs.
Each Modbus-Read node in Node-RED should be linked to an MQTT-out node to publish the acquired sensor data.

Configuring a Modbus client for Node-RED involves setting the type to TCP, specifying the host IP from the USR-W610, using port 502, defining the Unit ID as the SME's Modbus address, and setting a timeout of 4000 milliseconds with reconnection on timeout enabled.
diagram_labels: Figure 2: Modbus Address configuration in Rheonics Control Panel
warnings_notes: The LCD Display configuration is for SME-TRD Only, indicating a model-specific feature.
The default port for the Modbus client is 502.
The Modbus client configuration includes a 'Reconnect on timeout' setting, indicating a feature for connection stability.

chunks.page_summary: This page details Modbus-client node configuration steps for settings and queues, and outlines the MQTT topic naming convention for Rheonics SRV and SRD sensors.
chunks.extracted_text:
• Reconnect timeout (ms): 3000

Edit Modbus-Read node > Edit modbus-client node

[Delete button] [Cancel button] [Update button]

Properties [Settings tab selected] Queues Optionals

Name: RTU_SRV1
Type: TCP
Host: 192.168.100.73
Port: 502
TCP Type: DEFAULT
Unit-Id: 1
Timeout (ms): 4000
Reconnect on timeout: [checkbox checked]
Reconnect timeout (ms): 3000

Figure 5: Modbus-client node Settings configuration

4. On the Queues Tab, configure as follows. This is to avoid overwhelming the USR-W610 and read the measurements correctly.
• UnitId's in parallel queues: [checkbox checked]
• Queue commands: [checkbox checked]
• Queue delay (ms): 500

Edit Modbus-Read node > Edit modbus-client node

[Delete button] [Cancel button] [Update button]

Properties Settings [Queues tab selected] Optionals

UnitId's in parallel queues: [checkbox checked] Don't use it for serial without a serial gateway.
Queue Logging: [checkbox unchecked]
Queue commands: [checkbox checked]
Queue delay (ms): 500

Figure 6: Modbus-client node Queues configuration

5. Add a new modbus-client for each SME and configure as explained.

4.1. MQTT Topic Naming Convention

[URL: https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000419359-rheonics-sensor-integration-with-ipc-usr-w610-and-ignition-scada] To learn how to send Rheonics sensor data

To keep topics organized, follow a structured naming scheme. Ensure each sensor’s data is published to a unique MQTT topic to prevent conflicts and to make data handling easier on the Ignition Designer side. For this example, the format used is the following:
• SRV1
  ◦ viscosity: srv1/viscosity
  ◦ density: srv1/density
  ◦ temperature: srv1/temperature
• SRV2
  ◦ viscosity: srv2/viscosity
  ◦ density: srv2/density
  ◦ temperature: srv2/temperature
• SRV3
  ◦ viscosity: srv3/viscosity
  ◦ density: srv3/density
  ◦ temperature: srv3/temperature
• SRD
  ◦ viscosity: srd/viscosity
  ◦ density: srd/density
  ◦ temperature: srd/temperature
  ◦ kinviscosity: srd/kinviscosity
key_facts: The Modbus-client node's Queues Tab configuration prevents overwhelming the USR-W610 and ensures correct measurement reading.
Each sensor's data should be published to a unique MQTT topic to prevent conflicts and simplify data handling in Ignition Designer.
The MQTT topic naming convention follows `sensor_id/measurement_type`.
SRV sensors publish viscosity, density, and temperature data.
SRD sensors publish viscosity, density, temperature, and kinviscosity data.
diagram_labels: Don't use it for serial without a serial gateway.
warnings_notes: This is to avoid overwhelming the USR-W610 and read the measurements correctly.
Don't use it for serial without a serial gateway.

chunks.page_summary: This page describes how to configure and visualize Rheonics sensor data, including viscosity, density, and temperature, from multiple SRV and SRD sensors within Ignition SCADA using MQTT and Node-RED.
chunks.extracted_text:
5. Configuring Ignition SCADA MQTT
In Ignition SCADA, each SME can be linked to its custom namespace within the MQTT Engine module. This can help organize data by sensor and ensure clear identification across the interface.
• Name: For this example, each sensor's serial number was used as the namespace name and as the Root Tag Folder name.
• Subscriptions: Assign the MQTT topics to each namespace based on the naming structure used in Node-RED.
This image displays the MQTT Engine's configuration, showing how each sensor's topics are grouped into their namespace.

Figure 7: Node-RED flow with multiple sensors

Figure 8: MQTT Engine configuration

MQTT Engine Configuration Table:
Tabs: General, Servers, Sets, Namespaces
Sub-tabs: Default, Custom
Table Headers: Name, Subscriptions, Root Tag Folder, Tag Name, JSON Payload, Actions

Table Data:
Name: AO-0001, Subscriptions: srv1/viscosity,srv1/density,srv1/temperature, Root Tag Folder: A0-0001, Tag Name: (empty), JSON Payload: false, Actions: delete, edit
Name: AO-0002, Subscriptions: srv2/viscosity,srv2/density,srv2/temperature, Root Tag Folder: A0-0002, Tag Name: (empty), JSON Payload: false, Actions: delete, edit
Name: AO-0003, Subscriptions: srv3/viscosity,srv3/density,srv3/temperature, Root Tag Folder: A0-0003, Tag Name: (empty), JSON Payload: false, Actions: delete, edit
Name: AO-0004, Subscriptions: srd/viscosity,srd/density,srd/temperature,srd/kinviscosity, Root Tag Folder: A0-0004, Tag Name: (empty), JSON Payload: false, Actions: delete, edit
Name: TTN, Subscriptions: v3/srv-test@ttn/devices/rs485-converter/up, Root Tag Folder: TTN, Tag Name: (empty), JSON Payload: false, Actions: delete, edit

Link: Create new Custom Namespace...

6. Visualization in Ignition Designer
To learn how to connect Ignition SCADA to a MQTT broker to visualize Rheonics sensor data refer to Using Ignition SCADA to Visualize Rheonics Sensor Data via MQTT http://
Using the Rheonics templates available on Ignition Exchange, sensor data can be easily visualized. For this example, the existing display template was modified to have more SRV displays available to correctly assign each display to its respective tag.
The following figure shows the Tag Browser in Ignition Designer, where the sensor values are grouped under each namespace created previously, corresponding to each SME.
key_facts: Each Sensor Measurement Engine (SME) can be linked to a custom namespace within the MQTT Engine module in Ignition SCADA for data organization and clear identification.
The sensor's serial number is used as both the namespace name and the Root Tag Folder name in Ignition SCADA.
MQTT topics are assigned to each namespace based on the naming structure established in Node-RED.
Rheonics offers templates on Ignition Exchange to facilitate the visualization of sensor data.
An existing display template was modified to accommodate additional SRV displays for proper tag assignment.
Sensor values in the Ignition Designer's Tag Browser are grouped under namespaces, each corresponding to a specific SME.

7. References
*   Connecting Rheonics Sensors to Wi-Fi Networks using USR-W610 (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000419321-connecting-rheonics-sensors-to-wi-fi-networks-using-usr-w610)
*   Manuals (https://es.rheonics.com/recursos/manuales/)
*   Node-RED Setup and MQTT for Rheonics Sensor (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000418666-node-red-setup-and-mqtt-for-rheonics-sensors)
*   Modbus Input Registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297459-modbus-input-registers)
*   Integrating Rheonics Sensors with Node-RED and MQTT for Remote Data Access (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000418653-integrating-rheonics-sensors-with-node-red-and-mqtt-for-remote-data-access#2.4.-Add%20an-MQTT-Out-Node)
*   Using Ignition SCADA to Visualize Rheonics SME Data via MQTT (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000418655)
key_facts: Rheonics viscometers (SRV series) and density meters (SRD series) can be integrated and visualized using Ignition Designer software.
Data from Rheonics sensors, including density, temperature, kinematic viscosity, and viscosity, can be accessed and assigned to displays within Ignition Designer via an MQTT Engine.
The document demonstrates the transformation of display values in Ignition Designer after correctly assigning data tags to the respective displays.

Rheonics provides support and documentation for various integration topics, including connecting sensors to Wi-Fi using USR-W610, Node-RED and MQTT setup, Modbus input registers, and utilizing Ignition SCADA for data visualization.
The displayed sensor data includes viscosity in cP, temperature in °C, density in g/cc, and kinematic viscosity in cSt.
